old_claim.note: Writing is contextual infrastructure or shared knowledge, not a strict hard prerequisite.

new_claim.note: The scoped technology is a written law code; the Ur-Nammu witness is described as Sumerian text on clay in cuneiform, and Hammurabi's later laws were inscribed on a stela.

source_shape.source_locator: The Ur-Nammu Law Code, Description and Commentary: describes Sumerian text on clay in cuneiform and calls it the oldest known law code.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source identifies the Ur-Nammu law code as a written Sumerian/cuneiform law-code witness.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: For the narrowed concept of written law codes, writing is not merely context; it is part of the artifact definition.

ontology_before: The graph treated writing as a weak contextual dependency for a broad classical codified-law concept.
ontology_after: The graph treats writing as required for the narrowed ancient written-law-code scope.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: writing must remain a required prerequisite of codified_law.
would_reject_if: The node were broadened back to include oral customary law or legal institutions in general.
The source did not describe the law code as a written/cuneiform text.
A future split created separate oral-law and written-law-code nodes and moved this edge to the latter.
short_reason: A written law-code node requires writing by scope.
